title: Coreopsis crassifolia
intro: Coreopsis crassifolia, also known as tickseed or golden ragwort, is a perennial plant native to North America, characterized by its bright yellow flowers and thick, hairy foliage. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its purported health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which can help to soothe wounds, reduce pain, and prevent infection. Bioactive constituents of Coreopsis crassifolia include flavonoids, terpenoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various ailments, including skin conditions, digestive issues, and respiratory problems.
taxonomy: Coreopsis crassifolia is a species of flowering plant in the family Asteraceae. It belongs to the genus Coreopsis, which includes approximately 80 species of plants commonly known as tickseeds or coreopsis. Coreopsis crassifolia is native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The plant is a perennial herb that grows in dry to moist areas, often found in sandy or clay soils. As a member of the Asteraceae family, Coreopsis crassifolia produces showy flowers with yellow or orange petals.

Coreopsis crassifolia is also used in some traditional remedies to treat gastrointestinal issues and as a diuretic.
